text: In this video, we present the anatomical landmarks in oncological dissection for low rectal cancers. In the first case, we are looking at a patient with adenocarcinoma located at eight centimeters from the anal verge that has received neoadjuvant chemo in radiotherapy, for which we performed a low anterior rectal resection with total mesorectal excision.

The surgeon initiates posterior rectal dissection at the sacral promontory, entering the presacral space and identifying the proper rectal fascia and presacral fascia. The dissection proceeds in the avascular plane between the proper rectal and parietal pelvic fascia, moving superiorly and ventrally to incise the peritoneum at the superior margin of the rectovesical pouch, approaching the non-villiers fascia.

The surgeon performs a low posterior dissection starting at the fourth sacral vertebra, cutting through the thick retrosacral fascia to access the retrorectal space. Waldeyer's fascia is visible covering the floor of this space, including the anterior surface of the distal sacrum and the posterolateral pelvic muscles. The dissection continues with the detachment of the proporectal fascia from Waldeyer's fascia, which covers the levator ani muscle, and proceeds with an upper left lateral dissection between the proporectal and parietal pelvic fascia.

The surgeon performs a low anterior dissection by sectioning the non-villiers fascia at the base of the prostate, where it adheres to the prosthetic capsule. The dissection then shifts posteriorly to the non-villiers fascia, revealing the neurovascular bundles of Walsh. The rectal efferences of the inferior hypogastric plexus are identified and cut, allowing access to the levator ani muscles. The rectum is fully mobilized down to the level of the levator ani muscles below the prostate.

The surgeon sections the Valdia's fascia covering the anococcygeal raphe. The horizontal portion of the rectum is detached, achieving complete mobilization up to the puborectalis muscles, part of the levator ani muscle. At the anorectal junction, the rectum is transected using an endogia stapler, approximately three to four centimeters from the anal verge. The specimen is then retracted from the pelvis.

The pelvic floor is exposed, revealing the rectal stump below the prostate and the levator ani muscles. The neurovascular bundle and inferior hypogastric plexus are visible laterally. The surgeon checks the integrity of the nerves, including the superior hypogastric plexus, hypogastric nerves covered by the parietal pelvic fascia, inferior hypogastric plexus, and S-II to S-IV splanchnic nerves. A standard stapled colorectal anastomosis is performed.

The surgeon extracts the specimen through a suprapubic incision. The endoscopic view shows the anastomosis at the top of the anal canal, six months postoperatively. In the second patient, the tumor is in contact with the left puborectalis muscle, and a low anterior resection with puborectalis transection is performed, along with intrasphincteric resection extending into the deep part of the left external sphincter.

The surgeon performs high anterior dissection in front of the de Noveles fascia, which is thicker and adherent at the base of the prostate. The fascia is sectioned, and the dissection shifts posteriorly to the de Noveles fascia, contacting the proper rectal fascia to fully mobilize the rectum anteriorly. The visual difference between high and low anterior dissection is evident.

The cut edge of the de Noveles fascia is visible, adhering to the prostate. The surgeon sections the rectal deferent branches of the inferior hypogastric plexus to access the levator ani muscles. The rectal sacral fascia is identified and cut to enter the retrorectal space, and the last posterior attachment of the proper rectal fascia to the Valdia's fascia is severed.

The surgeon uses laparoscopic magnification to section the puborectalis muscle on the left side, posterolaterally, in the perineal region to achieve a safe circumferential margin. This approach is used in a patient with good preoperative sphincteric function.

The surgeon performs a standard intersphincteric dissection between the internal and external anal sphincters, starting at the dentate line posteriorly and laterally. The dissection is extended laterally on the left side to include the deep part of the external anal sphincter.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectum anteriorly, achieving a distal margin of at least one centimeter, confirmed by frozen sections. The specimen is extracted transanally using a pull-through technique. After preparing the meso and verifying the resection area, a standard end-to-end Park's manual colloidal anastomosis is performed. The postoperative view of the abdomen shows a temporary loop ileostomy. An endoscopic view reveals the anastomosis located one centimeter south-left from the anipotaneous line, six months post-surgery.

In the third case, the surgeon addresses a tumor invading the puborectalis muscle on the left side, both internal and external sphincters, and a five millimeter portion of the inferior vaginal wall. The procedure involves partial sectioning of the levator ani and a partial posterior colpectomy.

The surgeon sections the thickened Waldeyer's fascia posterolaterally to expose the fibers of the Levatorani muscle. Using a laparoscopic approach, the Levatorani is sectioned under direct vision to obtain a large safe circumferential margin in a cylindrical specimen, revealing the ischorectal adipose tissue. The posterior levator is sectioned in front of the coccyx.

On the left side, the surgeon cuts the levator muscle from its attachments at the internal obturator fascia due to tumor invasion. The rectum remains attached to the inferior portion of the vagina, corresponding to the area of tumoral invasion. A large circumferential incision is made to remove the sphincters, with the dissection mirroring the abdominal approach.

The surgeon resects the infiltrated posterior vaginal wall en bloc with the specimen, which includes the levator ani muscle. The specimen is transiently extracted, showing the intact proper rectal fascia. The final postoperative view reveals the closed perineum.
